texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Global warming is one of the most serious problems faced by earth at present. Anthropogenic CO2 is one of the gases causing this problem, and its main sources stem from fossil fuel based power production (Desideri and Paolucci, 1999), traffic, iron and steel, cement production and the metal production based on reduction with carbon. CO2 absorption in aqueous amine solutions is the most widely used process in the industry, and the most viable one at present. However, capital and operating costs are high, and in particular the heat required for absorbent regeneration. A better solvent may have higher CO2 loading capacity, lower heat of absorption, faster absorption kinetics and favorable environmental properties. A combination of a secondary diamine (PZ) and a sterically hindered amine (AMP) can be expected to have high cyclic loading capacity combined with fast kinetics and possibly low heat consumption. Already, some investigations of AMP systems have been performed such as density and excess molar volumes (Chan et al., 2002), surface tension (Vazquez et al., 1997), enthalpy (Arcis et al., 2007) and pilot study (Gabrielsen et al., 2007). Piperazine has a higher reaction rate than the primary alkanolamines, due to its cyclic and diamine structure, and is often considered as promoter (Bishnoi and Rochelle 2000; Xu et al., 1998), but also as single
texts.text: Global warming is one of the most serious problems faced by earth at present. Anthropogenic CO2 is one of the gases causing this problem, and its main sources stem from fossil fuel based power production (Desideri and Paolucci, 1999), traffic, iron and steel, cement production and the metal production based on reduction with carbon. CO2 absorption in aqueous amine solutions is the most widely used process in the industry, and the most viable one at present. However, capital and operating costs are high, and in particular the heat required for absorbent regeneration. A better solvent may have higher CO2 loading capacity, lower heat of absorption, faster absorption kinetics and favorable environmental properties. A combination of a secondary diamine (PZ) and a sterically hindered amine (AMP) can be expected to have high cyclic loading capacity combined with fast kinetics and possibly low heat consumption. Already, some investigations of AMP systems have been performed such as density and excess molar volumes (Chan et al., 2002), surface tension (Vazquez et al., 1997), enthalpy (Arcis et al., 2007) and pilot study (Gabrielsen et al., 2007). Piperazine has a higher reaction rate than the primary alkanolamines, due to its cyclic and diamine structure, and is often considered as promoter (Bishnoi and Rochelle 2000; Xu et al., 1998), but also as single

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, experimental data and a simplified vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) model for the absorption of CO2 into aqueous solutions of piperazine (PZ) activated 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) are reported. The purpose of the work was to find the AMP/PZ system with the highest concentration and cyclic capacity, which could be used in the industry without forming solid precipitations at operational temperatures. The effect of the AMP/PZ ratio and the total concentration level of amine was studied. The highest possible ratio of AMP/PZ, which does not form solid precipitates during the absorption of CO2 at 40 1 C (40 wt% amine), was identified. Considering the maximum loading found in the screening tests for AMP/PZ (3 þ 1.5 M) and for 30 wt% MEA systems, the AMP/PZ system has about 128% higher specific cyclic capacity if operating between 40 and 80 1 C, and almost twice the CO2 partial pressure at 120 1 C compared to MEA.
texts.text: In this work, experimental data and a simplified vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) model for the absorption of CO2 into aqueous solutions of piperazine (PZ) activated 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) are reported. The purpose of the work was to find the AMP/PZ system with the highest concentration and cyclic capacity, which could be used in the industry without forming solid precipitations at operational temperatures. The effect of the AMP/PZ ratio and the total concentration level of amine was studied. The highest possible ratio of AMP/PZ, which does not form solid precipitates during the absorption of CO2 at 40 1 C (40 wt% amine), was identified. Considering the maximum loading found in the screening tests for AMP/PZ (3 þ 1.5 M) and for 30 wt% MEA systems, the AMP/PZ system has about 128% higher specific cyclic capacity if operating between 40 and 80 1 C, and almost twice the CO2 partial pressure at 120 1 C compared to MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: amine (Plaza and Rochelle, 2010). Kinetics of CO2 in PZ solutions were described by Derks et al. (2006). Sun et al. (2005) used a second-order reaction model for CO2 and PZ, and a zwitterion mechanism for CO2 and AMP. They also found that small additions of PZ significantly increased the absorption rate. Samanta and Bandyopadhyay (2009) have suggested a reaction mechanism for this system. It has also been shown that solutions of 2 amines may increase the absorption rates and reduce the solvent regeneration energy requirement (Chakravarty et al., 1985; Seo and Hong, 1996). The VLE data for the AMP/PZ 3/1.5 system was Yang et al. (2010), but the temperature and the loading range were increased in this publication, and the data is fitted only with one function in the overall temperature and loading range.
texts.text: amine (Plaza and Rochelle, 2010). Kinetics of CO2 in PZ solutions were described by Derks et al. (2006). Sun et al. (2005) used a second-order reaction model for CO2 and PZ, and a zwitterion mechanism for CO2 and AMP. They also found that small additions of PZ significantly increased the absorption rate. Samanta and Bandyopadhyay (2009) have suggested a reaction mechanism for this system. It has also been shown that solutions of 2 amines may increase the absorption rates and reduce the solvent regeneration energy requirement (Chakravarty et al., 1985; Seo and Hong, 1996). The VLE data for the AMP/PZ 3/1.5 system was Yang et al. (2010), but the temperature and the loading range were increased in this publication, and the data is fitted only with one function in the overall temperature and loading range.

texts.text: 2. Experimental section

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Piperazine ( Z 98%) and AMP ( Z 97%), supplied by Fluka, were used for the experiments without further purification and dissolved in deionized water. The N2 (99.6%) and CO2 (99.999%) were supplied by AGA Gas GmbH.
texts.text: Piperazine ( Z 98%) and AMP ( Z 97%), supplied by Fluka, were used for the experiments without further purification and dissolved in deionized water. The N2 (99.6%) and CO2 (99.999%) were supplied by AGA Gas GmbH.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The analysis of CO2 in the liquid samples was done with the standard BaCl2 method, see Ma'mun et al. (2006). A minimum of two parallel analyses were run, and if the difference was 3% or more, further analyses were run. The loading accuracy is estimated to be 7 3.
texts.text: The analysis of CO2 in the liquid samples was done with the standard BaCl2 method, see Ma'mun et al. (2006). A minimum of two parallel analyses were run, and if the difference was 3% or more, further analyses were run. The loading accuracy is estimated to be 7 3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Screening tests
texts.text: 2.1. Screening tests

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For comparison of the absorption rate and the loading capacity with other solvents, a screening apparatus was used (Aronu et al., 2009). The results were compared to the 30 wt% (5 M) monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The absorption screening tests were performed at 40 7 0.1 1 C. The screening tests were perfomed in an apparatus as shown in Fig. 1. A mixture of N2 and CO2 passes through the amine, a part of the CO2 is absorbed and the outlet gas phase CO2 content is continuously recorded. From this an estimate of the absorption rate as function of time can be obtained as well as the capacity of the solvent The flows of N2 and CO2 were controlled by flow controllers (Bronkhorst High Tech -0.1% full scale), and these regulate also their mixing ratio. The CO2 flow was set to 0.5 Nl/min and the N2 flow to 4.5 Nl/min. This gave a 10% CO2 concentration in the inlet gas flow through the amine solution. The volume of absorbent solution was set to 750 ml. The CO2 content in the gas passing through the amine solution was measured by an IR detector (Rosemount Binos 100) and continuously logged by LabView software. An analyzer calibration run was performed before every measurement, and the two points used being pure N2 and 10% CO2. The program then registered the measured remaining CO2 in the gas stream, and the difference between 0.5 Nl/min and the registered value by the IR instrument was saved as a function of time. The measurements were stopped when the measured outlet gas CO2 concentration reached 95% of the initial value. After the end of absorption, a liquid sample was taken for measuring the CO2 concentration and after desorption a sample was taken again and the density was measured. The analysis of the liquid phase CO2 content served to control the mass balance. The desorption was performed at 80 1 C, and the stripping gas was 100% N2.
texts.text: For comparison of the absorption rate and the loading capacity with other solvents, a screening apparatus was used (Aronu et al., 2009). The results were compared to the 30 wt% (5 M) monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The absorption screening tests were performed at 40 7 0.1 1 C. The screening tests were perfomed in an apparatus as shown in Fig. 1. A mixture of N2 and CO2 passes through the amine, a part of the CO2 is absorbed and the outlet gas phase CO2 content is continuously recorded. From this an estimate of the absorption rate as function of time can be obtained as well as the capacity of the solvent The flows of N2 and CO2 were controlled by flow controllers (Bronkhorst High Tech -0.1% full scale), and these regulate also their mixing ratio. The CO2 flow was set to 0.5 Nl/min and the N2 flow to 4.5 Nl/min. This gave a 10% CO2 concentration in the inlet gas flow through the amine solution. The volume of absorbent solution was set to 750 ml. The CO2 content in the gas passing through the amine solution was measured by an IR detector (Rosemount Binos 100) and continuously logged by LabView software. An analyzer calibration run was performed before every measurement, and the two points used being pure N2 and 10% CO2. The program then registered the measured remaining CO2 in the gas stream, and the difference between 0.5 Nl/min and the registered value by the IR instrument was saved as a function of time. The measurements were stopped when the measured outlet gas CO2 concentration reached 95% of the initial value. After the end of absorption, a liquid sample was taken for measuring the CO2 concentration and after desorption a sample was taken again and the density was measured. The analysis of the liquid phase CO2 content served to control the mass balance. The desorption was performed at 80 1 C, and the stripping gas was 100% N2.

texts.text: 2.2. Vapor-liquid equilibrium tests (VLE)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the equilibrium measurements two types of equipment were used. One atmospheric unit operating at 40, 60 and 80 1 C, and a high pressure unit, operating up to 7 bar, for 100 and 120 1 C.
texts.text: For the equilibrium measurements two types of equipment were used. One atmospheric unit operating at 40, 60 and 80 1 C, and a high pressure unit, operating up to 7 bar, for 100 and 120 1 C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The athmospheric glass apparatus consisted of 3 reactors each filled with 100-150 ml of preloaded amine solution (No. 2, 3 and 4 in Fig. 2). A gas phase consisting mainly of N2 and CO2 was circulated through the bottles until both temperature and CO2 partial pressure were stabilized. For the measurements of the CO2 concentrations in

A gas phase consisting mainly of N2 and CO2 was circulated through the bottles until both temperature and CO2 partial pressure were stabilized. For the measurements of the CO2 concentrations in

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the vapor phase, 4 IR detectors were used (Rosemount Binos 100) with ranges 0-2000 ppm 7 20 ppm, 0-1% 7 0.01%, 0-5% 7 0.01% and up to 20% 7 0.1% (Aronu et al., 2011). The reactors were placed in a thermostated tank, and heated up to the required temperature. The glass tank No.1 was empty, and served for preventing back-flush and possible contamination of the IR detector. The temperature of reactor No 4 and the thermostated tank was monitored, and the gas phase measurement recording and the liquid phase sampling were done when equilibrium was reached. All the glass equipment was placed in a closed thermostated box. As mentioned, the vapor phase is circulated through the amine solution. After equilibration the vapor phase passes a cooler reducing the gas phase temperature to 1015 1 C before passing to the IR analyzer. The temperature is monitored and considered as the saturation temperature for water in gas. Calibrations of all four IR detectors were performed daily. The CO2 partial pressure in the equilibrium cell was determined by the procedure described by Ma'mun et al. (2007). A sample of the liquid phase was taken from the reactor No. 4.

The temperature of reactor No 4 and the thermostated tank was monitored, and the gas phase measurement recording and the liquid phase sampling were done when equilibrium was reached. All the glass equipment was placed in a closed thermostated box. As mentioned, the vapor phase is circulated through the amine solution. After equilibration the vapor phase passes a cooler reducing the gas phase temperature to 1015 1 C before passing to the IR analyzer. The temperature is monitored and considered as the saturation temperature for water in gas. Calibrations of all four IR detectors were performed daily. The CO2 partial pressure in the equilibrium cell was determined by the procedure described by Ma'mun et al. (2007). A sample of the liquid phase was taken from the reactor No. 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The apparatus for the high-temperature equilibrium measurements (Fig. 3) consisted of two vertically linked jacketed vessels with volumes 1.18 and 0.33. The small vessel was filled with ceramic Berl saddles. Thermostated heating oil served as heating medium. The vessels are connected and mounted in a framework, which allows them to be rotated 180 1 in a vertical plane so that the solvent can flow from one vessel to the other. The tilting frequency, 2 rpm, is governed by an electronically controlled pneumatic piston cylinder. The experiments were performed by first evacuation of the two cylinders, then purging with CO2, second evacuation and then about 150 ml of unloaded solution was allowed to pass into the small vessel. Then CO2 gas was added up to an approximate desired pressure When the set temperature was reached, the desired pressure was adjusted on the gas flask reduction valve, and the rotation was started. After one hour the CO2 feed was closed and 1-2 h were used for establishing and stabilizing equilibrium. The time needed was

The small vessel was filled with ceramic Berl saddles. Thermostated heating oil served as heating medium. The vessels are connected and mounted in a framework, which allows them to be rotated 180 1 in a vertical plane so that the solvent can flow from one vessel to the other. The tilting frequency, 2 rpm, is governed by an electronically controlled pneumatic piston cylinder. The experiments were performed by first evacuation of the two cylinders, then purging with CO2, second evacuation and then about 150 ml of unloaded solution was allowed to pass into the small vessel. Then CO2 gas was added up to an approximate desired pressure When the set temperature was reached, the desired pressure was adjusted on the gas flask reduction valve, and the rotation was started. After one hour the CO2 feed was closed and 1-2 h were used for establishing and stabilizing equilibrium. The time needed was

texts.label: text
texts.orig: indicated by a stable system pressure and temperature ( 7 0.2 1 C and 7 0.1 kPa). At equilibrium the total pressure was recorded and a liquid sample was withdrawn to measure the CO2 loading. For this purpose a closed metal cylinder with known weight of unloaded amine solution was used to avoid any CO2 loss. It was attached to the small cylinder, and the overpressure allowed the transfer of solution into the sampling cylinder. Afterwards the weight of sampling cylinder was measured again and from the known dilution, the concentration of CO2 in the original solution was calculated.
texts.text: indicated by a stable system pressure and temperature ( 7 0.2 1 C and 7 0.1 kPa). At equilibrium the total pressure was recorded and a liquid sample was withdrawn to measure the CO2 loading. For this purpose a closed metal cylinder with known weight of unloaded amine solution was used to avoid any CO2 loss. It was attached to the small cylinder, and the overpressure allowed the transfer of solution into the sampling cylinder. Afterwards the weight of sampling cylinder was measured again and from the known dilution, the concentration of CO2 in the original solution was calculated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. The screening tests
texts.text: 3.1. The screening tests

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The target was to find the amine concentration with the highest possible absorption capacity, absorption rate and cyclic capacity and with a low heat of reaction. The loading capacity is the maximum CO2 content absorbed by the solution at the absorption conditions. The cyclic capacity is the difference between the rich and the lean loading. This gives the very useful CO2 carrying capacity in a cyclic process between the measured conditions and it is always less than the loading capacity.
texts.text: The target was to find the amine concentration with the highest possible absorption capacity, absorption rate and cyclic capacity and with a low heat of reaction. The loading capacity is the maximum CO2 content absorbed by the solution at the absorption conditions. The cyclic capacity is the difference between the rich and the lean loading. This gives the very useful CO2 carrying capacity in a cyclic process between the measured conditions and it is always less than the loading capacity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The AMP/PZ forms precipitates with CO2 at higher amine and CO2 concentrations, and this was not desired. A list of the tested concentrations and the qualitative observations can be found in Table 1.
texts.text: The AMP/PZ forms precipitates with CO2 at higher amine and CO2 concentrations, and this was not desired. A list of the tested concentrations and the qualitative observations can be found in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From these observations it can be concluded that at high concentration of AMP, PZ or a high total amine concentration, the CO2 loading causes precipitation formation in the solution, and also the viscosity and stickiness increases. This may lead to operational problems. The concentrations AMP/PZ 5/1, 3/2, 4/1.5 clogged the parts of the laboratory glass apparatus with small cross sections, even during the absorption stage. In some cases, the ceramic sinter used in the reactor for bubble distribution could be blocked. The holes are quite small where the CO2 reacts with amine, and as the CO2 concentration here is the highest, the probability to form precipitate is higher. Also the viscosity of the solution plays a significant role, mainly for desorption at 80 1 C, and this could cause operational problems. The collection of the successful (finished without operational problems) screening tests can be found in Table 2.
texts.text: From these observations it can be concluded that at high concentration of AMP, PZ or a high total amine concentration, the CO2 loading causes precipitation formation in the solution, and also the viscosity and stickiness increases. This may lead to operational problems. The concentrations AMP/PZ 5/1, 3/2, 4/1.5 clogged the parts of the laboratory glass apparatus with small cross sections, even during the absorption stage. In some cases, the ceramic sinter used in the reactor for bubble distribution could be blocked. The holes are quite small where the CO2 reacts with amine, and as the CO2 concentration here is the highest, the probability to form precipitate is higher. Also the viscosity of the solution plays a significant role, mainly for desorption at 80 1 C, and this could cause operational problems. The collection of the successful (finished without operational problems) screening tests can be found in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aronu et al. (2009) measured the maximum loading at 40 1 C and 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure and cyclic capacity for 5 M MEA in an identical measurement. They found 0.518 mol CO2/mol amine and 0.271 mol CO2/mol amine, respectively. For 2.5 M AMP þ 0.5 M PZ system they found 0.671 mol CO2/mol amine as maximum loading and a cyclic capacity of 0.524 mol CO2/mol amine. This implies that the AMP/PZ 3/1.5 M system has around 30% higher loading capacity and 23% higher cyclic capacity than the AMP/PZ 2.5/0.5 M system per liter of solution. The comparison of these systems measured by the screening equipment is only semi-quantitative with regard to absorption rates as the bubble distribution can vary somewhat (Ma'mun et al., 2007). However, the equilibrium results should be precise.
texts.text: Aronu et al. (2009) measured the maximum loading at 40 1 C and 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure and cyclic capacity for 5 M MEA in an identical measurement. They found 0.518 mol CO2/mol amine and 0.271 mol CO2/mol amine, respectively. For 2.5 M AMP þ 0.5 M PZ system they found 0.671 mol CO2/mol amine as maximum loading and a cyclic capacity of 0.524 mol CO2/mol amine. This implies that the AMP/PZ 3/1.5 M system has around 30% higher loading capacity and 23% higher cyclic capacity than the AMP/PZ 2.5/0.5 M system per liter of solution. The comparison of these systems measured by the screening equipment is only semi-quantitative with regard to absorption rates as the bubble distribution can vary somewhat (Ma'mun et al., 2007). However, the equilibrium results should be precise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since reaction (2) includes CO2 in the gas phase, this describes the total absorption process. The heat of reaction may thus be denoted as the heat of absorption. Eq. (5) has been used extensively in the literature to find the heat of absorption from equilibrium data (Lee, 1976; Jou et al., 1982; Rebolledo-Libreros and Trejo, 2004). It must be emphasized that this is an approximation as changes in the activity coefficient and mol fraction ratios have been disregarded.
texts.text: Since reaction (2) includes CO2 in the gas phase, this describes the total absorption process. The heat of reaction may thus be denoted as the heat of absorption. Eq. (5) has been used extensively in the literature to find the heat of absorption from equilibrium data (Lee, 1976; Jou et al., 1982; Rebolledo-Libreros and Trejo, 2004). It must be emphasized that this is an approximation as changes in the activity coefficient and mol fraction ratios have been disregarded.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: P. Bru ´der et al. / Chemical Engineering Science 66 (2011) 6193-6198
texts.text: P. Bru ´der et al. / Chemical Engineering Science 66 (2011) 6193-6198

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The range of the model, based on the experimental data, is up to a loading of about 0.83 mol CO2/mol amine for the AMP/PZ system, and 0.57 for the 30 wt% MEA systems. The 5 M MEA data (Table 3) and the model in Eq. (6) are based on unpublished internal data. It shows good agreement between the experimental points and model over the whole range. It should be emphasized that this model is only a parameterised fit of the data and has no thermodynamic significance. The partial CO2 pressure for AMP/PZ at a loading of 0.58 mol CO2/mol amine - which is the maximum loading from the screening tests at 40 1 C, see Table 2 - and 120 1 C is about 600 kPa. Compared to 30 wt% MEA, where the same screening tests gave a maximum loading of 0.46 mol CO2/mol amine, the equilibrium CO2 pressure is about 358 kPa at the same temperature. This indicates that the AMP/PZ system has a potential for a significant reduction in stripping steam requirement during desorption as the CO2 partial pressure is much higher. From the screening tests it is also clear that the specific cyclic capacity, measured between 40 and 80 1 C, see Table 2, is also about twice as high, even when considering the 10% difference in total amine concentration between the two systems. This indicates that also the sensible heat loss will be significantly lower for the AMP/PZ system.
texts.text: The range of the model, based on the experimental data, is up to a loading of about 0.83 mol CO2/mol amine for the AMP/PZ system, and 0.57 for the 30 wt% MEA systems. The 5 M MEA data (Table 3) and the model in Eq. (6) are based on unpublished internal data. It shows good agreement between the experimental points and model over the whole range. It should be emphasized that this model is only a parameterised fit of the data and has no thermodynamic significance. The partial CO2 pressure for AMP/PZ at a loading of 0.58 mol CO2/mol amine - which is the maximum loading from the screening tests at 40 1 C, see Table 2 - and 120 1 C is about 600 kPa. Compared to 30 wt% MEA, where the same screening tests gave a maximum loading of 0.46 mol CO2/mol amine, the equilibrium CO2 pressure is about 358 kPa at the same temperature. This indicates that the AMP/PZ system has a potential for a significant reduction in stripping steam requirement during desorption as the CO2 partial pressure is much higher. From the screening tests it is also clear that the specific cyclic capacity, measured between 40 and 80 1 C, see Table 2, is also about twice as high, even when considering the 10% difference in total amine concentration between the two systems. This indicates that also the sensible heat loss will be significantly lower for the AMP/PZ system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The heat of absorption as a function of loading, as an average for the temperature interval between 40 and 120 1 C, can be calculated according to Eq. (5) and is shown in Table 4.
texts.text: The heat of absorption as a function of loading, as an average for the temperature interval between 40 and 120 1 C, can be calculated according to Eq. (5) and is shown in Table 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is known that the heat of absorption in 5 M MEA is about 83-84 kJ/mol CO2 absorbed, and that it is reasonably constant up to a loading of 0.5, after which it falls rapidly as the capacity of MEA vanishes (Kim and Svendsen, 2007). The heat of reaction for the AMP/PZ system is seen to be about the same as for MEA at lower loadings but decreases slowly as AMP takes over the absorption of CO2, and retains its capacity up to much higher loadings. This is because of the diamine structure of PZ and also because the carbamate formation of AMP is very weak. From the heat of absorption data it is clear that the AMP/PZ system does not offer any advantage over the MEA system in this respect.
texts.text: It is known that the heat of absorption in 5 M MEA is about 83-84 kJ/mol CO2 absorbed, and that it is reasonably constant up to a loading of 0.5, after which it falls rapidly as the capacity of MEA vanishes (Kim and Svendsen, 2007). The heat of reaction for the AMP/PZ system is seen to be about the same as for MEA at lower loadings but decreases slowly as AMP takes over the absorption of CO2, and retains its capacity up to much higher loadings. This is because of the diamine structure of PZ and also because the carbamate formation of AMP is very weak. From the heat of absorption data it is clear that the AMP/PZ system does not offer any advantage over the MEA system in this respect.
